what_it_does: Stores a JSON array of emergent design-pattern observations extracted from pitch-deck variants, each annotated with a category, name, recipe, evidence, prescriptive generator_rule, source deck, and depth variant.
observations: Each record maps a visual or structural convention to a semantic meaning and a generator rule.
Sources include 'capital-raise' 30-page (split into part 1 and part 2) and 'company-info' 18-page.
Patterns range from high-level narrative devices (title chain, appendix density progression) to atomic glyphs (bullet triangles, status dots, arrow directions).
Many rules encode meaning into color, opacity, shape, or arrow direction rather than using decoration.
The file is truncated mid-object at the end, cutting off the final 'Intentional text length matching' entry.
Some categories are reused for unrelated patterns, e.g. 'gradient-shading-opacity' covers both stat bands and background watermarks.
Green and red semantics differ across sources: capital-raise reserves them for directional trends, company-info allows green for active status.
standouts: Slide titles form a single continuous grammatical sentence across the deck.
Gold is the sole attention signal; near-black and gray are passive or receding.
Fixed shape vocabulary: hexagon = Property Wizard, octagon = Virtual Tours, etc.
Arrow direction carries grammar: right = forward/capability, left = inbound result, up/down = trend.
Design language scales across 1-page, 15-page, and 30-page compression levels.
conventions: Follows a consistent per-entry schema: category, name, recipe, evidence, emergent flag, generator_rule, _src, _depth.
Evidence is anchored to slide references (p-02, p16, etc.).
Generator rules are prescriptive and often use absolute language ('Never', 'Always', 'Reserve').
Breaks completeness: the final object is truncated.
Breaks category uniqueness: the same category label groups semantically different patterns.
concerns: File ends abruptly, losing the tail of the last record.
Evidence cites slide numbers without any slide content or image in the file itself.
Some 'NEVER' rules appear source-specific and may conflict with other source rules in the same file.
The 'emergent' flag is always true, so it does not distinguish degrees of confidence.
A few patterns rely on inferred rather than directly shown evidence.
notes: A source-tagged design-pattern ledger that turns observed pitch-deck conventions into prescriptive generator rules, useful for consistency checking but truncated near the end.
questions: What ingestion process generated these records and how was 'emergent' determined?
How should conflicting rules across source decks be resolved?
Is the truncated final object available in another file or chunk?
What do _src and _depth map to in the broader project structure?
Are the generator rules validated against actual slide assets?
purpose_vs_actual: The file appears intended to capture emergent, reusable design-language rules; it does so as a pattern dictionary with generator rules, not as an interpretive essay.
apparent_themes: Design language as a formal grammar
intention_signals: It is trying to codify the pitch deck's visual and rhetorical grammar into reusable, enforceable rules so future decks can reproduce the same design language at any compression level.
novelty: Treats slide-deck design as a rule-based language with connective title chains, directional arrow semantics, fixed shape-role mappings, and depth-compression guidelines.
contribution: Provides a structured, evidence-anchored catalog of visual semantics and prescriptive generator rules that a fused system can use to preserve or audit design-language consistency across deck variants.
summary_for_embedding: This file is a JSON array of emergent design-pattern records extracted from pitch-deck variants, each describing a visual convention, its evidence, and a prescriptive generator rule, organized by category and tagged with source deck and depth variant.
intention_for_embedding: It was created to capture reusable, source-aware design-language rules so that deck generation or review can reproduce observed semantic-visual conventions.
